metadata.title: Silencing the Doctrine of Discovery – The Brazilian Process: Accidental Discoveries, Secret Manuscripts, Imaginary Lines and Myths
metadata.description: Alencar traces how the Doctrine of Discovery shaped Brazil through church-backed silence, racial myths, and colonial violence, urging decolonial accountability.

metadata.title: Deconstructing the Erie Canal: Three Lessons for its Next Century
metadata.description: In this bicentennial reflection on the Erie Canal, Renee Barry examines how celebratory public histories mask the canal's foundation in settler colonial violence on unceded Haudenosaunee land. Drawing on archival research, heritage tourism analysis, and museum narratives, the essay deconstructs myths of progress, civilization, and national destiny embedded in iconic commemorations such as the Wedding of the Waters. Barry argues that these narratives normalize environmental damage, erase Indigenous sovereignty, and recast genocidal dispossession as American achievement. The article links canal ideology to Christian dominance, European expansion, and the legal legacy of the Doctrine of Discovery, while also tracing how revivalist and reform movements reproduced similar hierarchies. Blending critical history with self-reflection, Barry calls for a different future in which Erie Canal memory is reoriented around Indigenous leadership, sacred relationships to place, and accountable ecological repair rather than triumphalist nostalgia. She urges institutions, educators, and visitors to confront inherited narratives and support decolonial stewardship in practice.

metadata.title: Unselling the Classroom: Confronting History and Ourselves
metadata.description: Berlin urges teachers to confront settler colonialism and white supremacy by centering Indigenous history critical pedagogy, and accountability today.

metadata.title: The Medieval Origins of Religious White Supremacy: English Imperialism, Crusade Defeats, and the Doctrine of Discovery
metadata.description: Callan traces how medieval English myths, crusade defeat, and Irish colonization shaped Christian white supremacy and fed the global Doctrine of Discovery.

metadata.title: Expecting Excellence in Education: When Content Conditions Class Consciousness
metadata.description: Chaness links white supremacy, settler colonialism, and anti-Indian racism, showing how Indigenous values and pedagogy reshape critical classroom practice.

metadata.title: The Chosen People at Grouse Mountain
metadata.description: Felese challenges conquest-based land values and shows Indigenous relational worldviews offer life-affirming alternatives to extraction and alienation

metadata.title: Flesh of Words: Confrontation, Navigation, and Integrity in the English Classroom
metadata.description: Hurtado uses Critical Race Theory and Latina feminisms to show how multiethnic curricula can confront colonial legacies and teach resistance in class.

metadata.title: Introduction
metadata.description: Introduction to a global volume on Christian Discovery, linking law, religion, and pedagogy, with Indigenous sovereignty and decolonial justice today.

metadata.title: Dismantling White Supremacy in the Classroom and Beyond
metadata.description: Jimenez shows criminal justice education must confront white supremacy by centering race, power and oppression to transform teaching and policies now.

metadata.title: Other Forms of Dwelling: A Dalit – Feminist Perspective
metadata.description: Lakshmi frames Dalit feminist values alongside Indigenous frameworks to show alternative forms of dwelling, relation, and resistance beyond colonial modernity.

metadata.title: Charting the Doctrine in the Colonial Archive: Papal Bulls and the Translation of the ‘Discovery’ Purpose
metadata.description: Modrow shows how papal bulls transformed crusade theology into global colonial strategy, legitimizing Indigenous dispossession and imperial expansion.

metadata.title: “Engineering Marvel”: Towards Resisting the Affective Politics of Erie Canal Heritage
metadata.description: Nagle critiques Erie Canal heritage marketing, showing how engineered marvel obscures Haudenosaunee dispossession and calls settlers to affective resistance.

metadata.title: Using the Doctrine of Discovery to Increase Shared Language and Conceptual Frameworks Between Black and Indigenous Feminist Organizing
metadata.description: Nahar argues Doctrine of Discovery can build shared language between Black and Indigenous feminisms, strengthening solidarity against settler colonial power.

metadata.title: A Preface to Challenging the Justifications of Domination Through Religion: “We Were Planting Corn, and They Were Planting Crosses”
metadata.description: Preface to a special issue examining Christian Discovery’s role in white supremacy, law, and education, with decolonial paths grounded in Indigenous justice.

metadata.title: Schools, Teachers, and Teacher Educators: Education Through the Disruption of White Supremacy
metadata.description: Radhakrishnan examines how U.S. schooling reproduces white supremacy and identifies teacher education strategies to disrupt curriculum, instruction, and policy.

metadata.title: Hindu Political Theology: Beyond Hindutva’s Political Monotheism
metadata.description: Somayajula reads Hindutva as political theology, showing how Hindu nationalism flattens religious diversity and urging a more inclusive Hindu identity.

metadata.title: Baltic Religion: The Sacred Things
metadata.description: Trinkauskaite explores Baltic sacred traditions and sutartinės, linking domestic deities and revivalist practice to collective ethics beyond hierarchy.

affiliations.name: Matilda Joslyn Gage Center and Syracuse University

metadata.title: Christian Control of Women and Mother Earth: The Doctrine of Discovery and the Doctrine of Male Domination
metadata.description: Wagner links church patriarchy and the Doctrine of Discovery to colonial violence, calling for Indigenous rematriation to restore women and the Earth.
